title: The Creation of Adam and Eve
description: God created the first man, Adam, from the dust of the ground and placed him in the Garden of Eden. He then created Eve from Adam's rib as his companion. They lived in perfect harmony with God until the Fall.

title: The Fall of Man
description: Adam and Eve disobeyed God by eating fruit from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil, after being tempted by the serpent. Their sin brought death and separation from God into the world, and they were expelled from Eden.

title: Expulsion from the Garden
description: After the Fall, God placed cherubim and a flaming sword at the east of Eden to guard the way to the Tree of Life, preventing Adam and Eve from eating its fruit and living forever in their sinful state.

title: Noah's Ark Comes to Rest
description: After 150 days of flooding that covered the entire earth, God caused a wind to pass over the waters. The ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat as the floodwaters receded, marking the end of God's judgment and the beginning of a renewed creation.

title: The Tower of Babel
description: Humanity gathered on the plain of Shinar to build a city and a tower reaching to the heavens, seeking to make a name for themselves. God confused their language, scattering them across the earth and ending their unified rebellion.

title: The Call of Abraham
description: God called Abraham (then Abram) while he lived in Ur of the Chaldeans, commanding him to leave his country, his people, and his father's household to go to a land that God would show him. This began the covenant relationship between God and the nation of Israel.

title: Abraham's Stay in Haran
description: Terah took his family from Ur to Haran, where they settled. After Terah's death, God renewed His call to Abraham in Haran, and Abraham departed for Canaan at age 75, taking his nephew Lot with him.

title: Destruction of Sodom
description: God rained down brimstone and fire from heaven upon Sodom and Gomorrah, destroying the cities and all their inhabitants because of their great wickedness. Lot and his family were rescued by angels, but Lot's wife looked back and became a pillar of salt.

title: Destruction of Gomorrah
description: Gomorrah was destroyed alongside Sodom by fire from heaven. The cities became a perpetual example of God's judgment against sin, frequently referenced throughout Scripture as a warning.

title: Abraham Settles in Hebron
description: Abraham moved his family to the plain of Mamre near Hebron, where he built an altar to the Lord. Hebron became his primary residence, and it was here that God reaffirmed His covenant promise that Abraham would have descendants as numerous as the stars.

title: The Cave of Machpelah
description: Abraham purchased the Cave of Machpelah near Hebron to bury his wife Sarah. This became the family tomb where Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah were also buried — the only piece of the Promised Land that Abraham personally owned.

title: Abraham's Covenant at Beersheba
description: Abraham made a covenant with Abimelech at Beersheba over a well that Abraham had dug. He planted a tamarisk tree there and called upon the name of the Lord. The well gave the city its name, meaning 'Well of the Oath' or 'Well of Seven.'

title: Jacob's Vision at Beersheba
description: Jacob offered sacrifices to God at Beersheba on his way to Egypt. God spoke to him in visions of the night, reassuring him not to fear going down to Egypt, promising to make him a great nation there and to bring him back again.

title: Jacob's Ladder at Bethel
description: While fleeing from his brother Esau, Jacob stopped for the night at Bethel. He dreamed of a ladder reaching from earth to heaven with angels ascending and descending on it. God stood above it and renewed His covenant promises to Jacob. When Jacob awoke, he declared, 'This is the house of God.'

title: Jacob Returns to Bethel
description: Years later, God commanded Jacob to return to Bethel and build an altar. Jacob purified his household, removing all foreign gods, and built an altar at Bethel. God appeared to him again and confirmed his new name, Israel, renewing the covenant promises.

title: The Giving of the Ten Commandments
description: Moses ascended Mount Sinai where God descended in fire and smoke. God gave Moses the Ten Commandments written on tablets of stone, establishing His covenant with Israel. The mountain quaked greatly and the people witnessed thunder, lightning, and the sound of a trumpet.

title: The Golden Calf
description: While Moses was on Mount Sinai receiving the Law, the people grew impatient and persuaded Aaron to make a golden calf to worship. God's anger burned against them, and Moses broke the tablets of the Law when he descended and saw their idolatry.

title: The Tabernacle Instructions
description: On Mount Sinai, God gave Moses detailed instructions for building the Tabernacle — a portable sanctuary where God would dwell among His people. The plans included the Ark of the Covenant, the altar, the lampstand, and the priesthood garments.

title: The Parting of the Red Sea
description: Moses stretched out his hand over the Red Sea, and God drove back the sea with a strong east wind, turning it into dry land. The Israelites crossed on dry ground with walls of water on either side. When the Egyptian army pursued, the waters returned and drowned them all.

title: Water from the Rock at Meribah
description: In the Wilderness of Zin at Kadesh, the people complained of thirst. God told Moses to speak to the rock to bring forth water, but Moses struck it twice with his staff in anger. Water flowed abundantly, but Moses' disobedience prevented him from entering the Promised Land.

title: Spies Sent from Kadesh-Barnea
description: From the Wilderness of Paran, Moses sent twelve spies to explore the Promised Land. They returned after 40 days with a report of a land flowing with milk and honey, but ten spies spread fear about the inhabitants. Only Joshua and Caleb trusted God, and the people's unbelief led to 40 years of wandering.

title: Crossing the Jordan River
description: Joshua led the Israelites across the Jordan River into the Promised Land. The priests carrying the Ark of the Covenant stepped into the water, and the river stopped flowing, piling up in a heap upstream. The people crossed on dry ground, just as they had crossed the Red Sea.

title: The Fall of Jericho
description: Joshua led the Israelites in a unique battle against Jericho. For six days, the army marched around the city once each day with the priests blowing trumpets. On the seventh day, they marched seven times, the priests blew their trumpets, the people shouted, and the walls collapsed flat.

title: Healing of Blind Bartimaeus
description: As Jesus was leaving Jericho with His disciples, blind Bartimaeus sat by the roadside begging. He cried out, 'Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!' Jesus called him forward and restored his sight, saying, 'Your faith has healed you.' Bartimaeus immediately followed Jesus.

title: Jesus and Zacchaeus
description: In Jericho, Jesus encountered Zacchaeus, a wealthy tax collector who was short in stature and climbed a sycamore tree to see Him. Jesus called him down and went to his house, prompting criticism from the crowd. Zacchaeus repented, promising to give half his possessions to the poor.

title: The Conquest of Ai
description: After the sin of Achan caused Israel's initial defeat at Ai, God instructed Joshua to set an ambush. Joshua sent 30,000 men to hide behind the city while he feigned retreat, drawing the men of Ai out. The ambush captured the city and burned it, making it a permanent ruin.

title: The Gibeonite Deception
description: The Gibeonites, fearing Israel's conquest, disguised themselves as travelers from a distant country and tricked Joshua into making a peace treaty with them. When the deception was discovered, the Israelites honored the treaty but made the Gibeonites servants as woodcutters and water carriers.

title: The Sun Stands Still
description: When five Amorite kings attacked Gibeon, Joshua came to their aid. Joshua prayed to the Lord, and God caused hailstones to strike down the enemy. Then Joshua commanded the sun to stand still over Gibeon and the moon over the Valley of Aijalon, and the sun stopped in the middle of the sky for about a full day while Israel defeated its enemies.

title: The Tabernacle at Shiloh
description: After the conquest of Canaan, the Tabernacle was set up at Shiloh, which became Israel's religious center for over 300 years. All the tribes gathered there, and it was where the land was divided among them by lot. The Ark of the Covenant remained at Shiloh until it was captured by the Philistines.

title: Samuel's Calling at Shiloh
description: The young Samuel served the Lord under Eli the priest at Shiloh. One night, God called Samuel three times. Samuel finally responded, 'Speak, for Your servant is listening,' and received his first prophecy — a message of judgment against Eli's household for their wickedness.

title: Israel's First Camp in Canaan
description: Gilgal was the first camp of the Israelites after crossing the Jordan River. Here, Joshua set up twelve stones taken from the riverbed as a memorial. At Gilgal, the new generation was circumcised, the Passover was celebrated, and the manna ceased as the people began eating the produce of the land.

title: Saul Made King at Gilgal
description: After Saul's victory over the Ammonites, Samuel gathered all the people at Gilgal and officially confirmed Saul as king before the Lord. They offered peace offerings and rejoiced, though Samuel warned them about the consequences of having a human king.

title: Jeroboam's Golden Calf at Bethel
description: After the kingdom divided, King Jeroboam set up golden calves in Bethel and Dan to prevent the people from going to Jerusalem to worship. Bethel became a center of idolatry for the northern kingdom, and prophets repeatedly condemned its false worship.

title: The Ark in the Temple of Dagon
description: The Philistines captured the Ark of the Covenant and placed it in the temple of their god Dagon in Ashdod. The next morning, Dagon's statue had fallen face-down before the Ark. After being set upright, Dagon fell again with its head and hands broken off. God also afflicted the people of Ashdod with tumors.

title: David and Goliath
description: The Philistine champion Goliath of Gath, a giant over nine feet tall, challenged the armies of Israel for 40 days. The young shepherd David faced Goliath with only a sling and five smooth stones, declaring, 'The battle is the Lord's.' David struck Goliath with a stone and cut off his head, routing the Philistine army.

title: Samson Carries Away Gaza's Gates
description: Samson went to Gaza and spent the night with a prostitute. When the Gazites planned to kill him at sunrise, Samson rose at midnight, tore out the city gate doors and gateposts, and carried them on his shoulders to the top of a hill near Hebron.

title: The Death of Samson
description: After his hair was cut and his strength left him, Samson was captured by the Philistines, blinded, and imprisoned in Gaza. At a festival to their god Dagon, Samson was brought out to entertain them. He prayed for strength one last time and pushed down the temple pillars, killing himself and thousands of Philistines.

title: David Anointed King in Hebron
description: After Saul's death, David was anointed king over the tribe of Judah in Hebron. He ruled from Hebron for seven and a half years before conquering Jerusalem and establishing his capital there. During this time, his sons were born and his kingdom grew stronger.

title: Elijah's Victory on Mount Carmel
description: The prophet Elijah challenged 450 prophets of Baal to a contest on Mount Carmel. Two altars were prepared, and the prophets of Baal called on their god from morning until noon with no response. Elijah drenched his altar with water, prayed, and fire came down from heaven, consuming the sacrifice, wood, stones, and water. The people declared, 'The Lord, He is God!'

title: Blessings Pronounced from Mount Gerizim
description: When the Israelites entered the Promised Land, six tribes stood on Mount Gerizim to pronounce blessings for obedience to God's Law, while six tribes stood on Mount Ebal to pronounce curses for disobedience. This ceremony fulfilled Moses' instructions in Deuteronomy.

title: Curses Pronounced from Mount Ebal
description: Joshua built an altar on Mount Ebal as Moses had commanded, and the Levites recited the curses of the Law while the people responded 'Amen.' The ceremony demonstrated that obedience brings blessing and disobedience brings judgment.

title: David Conquers Jerusalem
description: David led his men to conquer the Jebusite stronghold of Jerusalem, which had remained unconquered since the time of Joshua. Through a daring attack via the water shaft, Joab and David's men captured the city, and David made Jerusalem his capital, bringing the Ark of the Covenant there.

title: Solomon Builds the Temple
description: Solomon built the First Temple on Mount Moriah in Jerusalem, the site where Abraham had offered Isaac. The Temple was a magnificent structure of stone and cedar, overlaid with gold, housing the Ark of the Covenant. When the Temple was dedicated, God's glory filled the building in the form of a cloud.

title: The Crucifixion of Jesus
description: Jesus was crucified outside Jerusalem at Golgotha. He was mocked, beaten, and nailed to a cross between two thieves. From noon until three in the afternoon, darkness covered the land. Jesus cried out, 'It is finished,' and gave up His spirit. The temple veil was torn in two, and the earth shook.

title: The Resurrection of Jesus
description: On the third day after His crucifixion, the tomb near Jerusalem was found empty. An angel rolled away the stone, and Jesus appeared alive to Mary Magdalene and other disciples. He walked, ate, and taught for 40 days before His ascension, providing many convincing proofs of His resurrection.

title: Pentecost and the Birth of the Church
description: In Jerusalem during the Feast of Pentecost, the Holy Spirit descended upon the apostles with the sound of a rushing wind and tongues of fire resting on them. They spoke in various languages, and Peter preached to the crowd, resulting in about 3,000 people being baptized and added to the church.

title: Jesus Crucified at Golgotha
description: Jesus was led to Golgotha (the Place of the Skull) outside Jerusalem where He was crucified between two criminals. He spoke seven final sayings from the cross, including 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing,' and 'It is finished.'

title: The Ascension of Jesus
description: After His resurrection, Jesus led His disciples to the Mount of Olives, blessed them, and was taken up into heaven before their eyes. A cloud received Him, and two angels appeared promising that He would return in the same way. The disciples returned to Jerusalem with great joy to await the Holy Spirit.

title: The Olivet Discourse
description: From the Mount of Olives, Jesus delivered His Olivet Discourse, a prophecy about the destruction of Jerusalem and the end of the age. He warned of false messiahs, wars, famines, and earthquakes, and He taught about His future return in power and glory.

title: The Birth of Jesus
description: Jesus Christ was born in Bethlehem to the Virgin Mary, fulfilling the prophecy of Micah. Because of a census ordered by Caesar Augustus, Joseph and Mary traveled to Bethlehem, and Jesus was born in a stable because there was no room in the inn. Shepherds came to worship Him after angels announced His birth.

title: The Annunciation to Mary
description: In Nazareth, the angel Gabriel appeared to the Virgin Mary and announced that she would conceive a son by the Holy Spirit. She would name Him Jesus, and He would be called the Son of the Most High, inheriting the throne of David and reigning forever over an everlasting kingdom.

title: Jesus Rejected in Nazareth
description: Jesus returned to His hometown of Nazareth and taught in the synagogue, reading from the scroll of Isaiah about the Spirit of the Lord being upon Him. The people marveled at His gracious words but then questioned, 'Isn't this Joseph's son?' They rejected Him, and He could do few miracles there because of their unbelief.

title: Jesus' Galilean Ministry
description: Jesus began His public ministry in Galilee after John the Baptist was imprisoned. He preached, 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near,' and called His first disciples — Peter, Andrew, James, and John — from their fishing boats on the Sea of Galilee. He taught in synagogues throughout the region.

title: Jesus Walks on Water
description: After feeding the 5,000, Jesus sent His disciples ahead by boat across the Sea of Galilee while He went to pray. During the night, a storm arose and the disciples struggled against the wind. Jesus walked to them on the water, telling them, 'Take courage! It is I. Do not be afraid.' Peter briefly walked on water before doubting.

title: Jesus Calms the Storm
description: While crossing the Sea of Galilee with His disciples, a furious storm arose with waves swamping the boat. Jesus was sleeping in the stern, and the terrified disciples woke Him. He rebuked the wind and the waves, saying, 'Peace! Be still!' and the sea became completely calm.

title: Calling the First Disciples
description: Jesus walked along the Sea of Galilee and called two brothers, Simon Peter and Andrew, who were casting nets into the sea, saying, 'Follow Me, and I will make you fishers of men.' Further along, He called James and John, who were mending their nets with their father Zebedee.

title: Healing of the Centurion's Servant
description: In Capernaum, a Roman centurion came to Jesus asking Him to heal his paralyzed servant. When Jesus offered to come to his house, the centurion expressed remarkable faith, saying he was unworthy and that Jesus only needed to speak the word. Jesus marveled at his faith, saying He had not found such faith in all Israel.

title: Jesus' Headquarters in Capernaum
description: Capernaum became the center of Jesus' Galilean ministry. He taught regularly in the synagogue, healed many people, and performed numerous miracles there. Jesus called the city 'His own city.' Despite witnessing many miracles, Capernaum was later rebuked for its unbelief.

title: The Wedding at Cana
description: Jesus performed His first miracle at a wedding in Cana of Galilee. When the host ran out of wine, Jesus' mother Mary told the servants to do whatever Jesus said. He instructed them to fill six stone water jars with water, then serve it to the master of the banquet, who declared it the best wine.

title: The Samaritan Woman at the Well
description: Jesus traveled through Samaria and rested at Jacob's Well near Sychar. He spoke with a Samaritan woman, breaking social barriers of ethnicity and gender. He revealed His identity as the Messiah and offered her 'living water.' Many Samaritans believed because of her testimony and Jesus' words.

title: The Raising of Lazarus
description: Jesus raised His friend Lazarus from the dead in Bethany. Lazarus had been buried for four days when Jesus arrived. Jesus called out, 'Lazarus, come out!' and Lazarus emerged from the tomb still wrapped in grave clothes. This great miracle caused many Jews to believe in Jesus but also intensified the plot to kill Him.

title: Jesus Anointed at Bethany
description: While Jesus was dining at the home of Simon the Leper in Bethany, a woman (identified as Mary, sister of Lazarus) poured a jar of expensive nard perfume on Jesus' feet and wiped them with her hair. Judas Iscariot objected to the waste, but Jesus commended her act as preparation for His burial.

title: Jesus Passes Through Jericho
description: On His final journey to Jerusalem, Jesus passed through Jericho. He healed a blind beggar named Bartimaeus and also encountered Zacchaeus, causing a transformation in the tax collector's life. This demonstrated Jesus' mission to seek and save the lost.

title: The Baptism of Jesus
description: John the Baptist baptized Jesus in the Jordan River. As Jesus came up from the water, heaven opened, the Holy Spirit descended on Him like a dove, and a voice from heaven said, 'This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.' This event marked the beginning of Jesus' public ministry.

title: The Exodus from Egypt
description: After 430 years of slavery in Egypt, God sent Moses to demand Pharaoh release the Israelites. Through ten devastating plagues, including the death of the firstborn, Pharaoh finally relented. The Israelites left Egypt in haste, taking unleavened bread, and God led them with a pillar of cloud by day and fire by night.

title: The Institution of the Passover
description: On the night before the Exodus, God instituted the Passover. Each Israelite family was to slaughter a lamb and mark their doorposts with its blood so the angel of death would 'pass over' their homes. They were to eat the lamb with unleavened bread and bitter herbs, commemorating their hasty departure from Egypt.

title: The Flight to Egypt
description: After the wise men departed, an angel warned Joseph in a dream to flee to Egypt because King Herod was seeking to kill the infant Jesus. Joseph, Mary, and Jesus escaped to Egypt and remained there until Herod's death, fulfilling the prophecy, 'Out of Egypt I called My Son.'

title: The Babylonian Exile
description: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon besieged Jerusalem, destroyed the Temple, and carried the people of Judah into exile in Babylon. The exile lasted 70 years as prophesied by Jeremiah. Daniel, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego served in Nebuchadnezzar's court, remaining faithful to God.

title: Daniel in the Lions' Den
description: Daniel, a high official in the Persian administration of Babylon, remained faithful to God despite a decree forbidding prayer to anyone but King Darius. He was thrown into the lions' den, but God sent an angel to shut the lions' mouths. King Darius then decreed that all should fear the God of Daniel.

title: Jonah's Mission to Nineveh
description: God commanded Jonah to preach against wicked Nineveh, but Jonah fled on a ship to Tarshish. After being swallowed by a great fish and repenting, Jonah finally went to Nineveh. His message caused the entire city — from the king to the animals — to repent in sackcloth and ashes, and God relented from destroying them.

title: The Conversion of Saul
description: On the road to Damascus, Saul of Tarsus — a fierce persecutor of Christians — was struck down by a brilliant light from heaven. He heard the voice of Jesus asking, 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute Me?' Blinded for three days, Saul was healed by Ananias, baptized, and became Paul, the greatest missionary in church history.

title: Believers First Called Christians in Antioch
description: In Antioch, a thriving multicultural church grew as believers scattered by persecution preached to both Jews and Greeks. Barnabas brought Saul (Paul) from Tarsus to help teach, and they met with the church for a full year. It was in Antioch that disciples were first called 'Christians.'

title: Paul's Imprisonment in Rome
description: Paul arrived in Rome under house arrest after appealing to Caesar. For two years, he lived in his own rented house, welcoming all who came to him and preaching the kingdom of God with boldness. During this time, he wrote several New Testament letters, including Ephesians, Philippians, and Colossians.

title: Paul's Ministry in Ephesus
description: Paul spent three years in Ephesus, more than anywhere else during his missionary journeys. He taught daily in the school of Tyrannus, performed extraordinary miracles, and many people turned from sorcery to faith. The spread of the gospel threatened the local idol-making trade, sparking a riot led by silversmiths of Artemis.

title: Paul Plants the Church in Corinth
description: Paul arrived in Corinth and met Aquila and Priscilla, tentmakers like himself. He preached in the synagogue every Sabbath and many Corinthians believed and were baptized. Despite opposition, Paul stayed 18 months, establishing one of the most significant early churches, though one that faced many challenges.

title: Paul's Sermon at the Areopagus
description: In Athens, Paul was deeply distressed by the city's idolatry. He was invited to speak at the Areopagus (Mars Hill), the intellectual center of Athens. Paul cleverly referenced the Athenians' altar to 'An Unknown God' and proclaimed the resurrection of Jesus, receiving a mixed response — some mocked, while others believed.

title: Paul and Silas in the Philippian Jail
description: In Philippi, Paul cast a demon out of a slave girl, leading to her masters dragging Paul and Silas before the authorities. They were beaten and imprisoned. At midnight, they prayed and sang hymns, and a violent earthquake opened all the prison doors. The terrified jailer asked, 'What must I do to be saved?' and believed.

title: John Receives the Revelation
description: John was exiled on the island of Patmos for his testimony of Jesus Christ. On the Lord's Day, he received a vision of the risen Christ and was commanded to write what he saw. The Book of Revelation contains John's visions of the seven churches, end-times judgments, the return of Christ, and the new heaven and new earth.

title: The Dead Sea and Sodom's Destruction
description: The region around the Dead Sea, known as the Valley of Siddim, was the location of Sodom and Gomorrah. After their destruction, the area became a barren salt waste. The Dead Sea, the lowest point on earth, serves as a geographical reminder of God's judgment and is frequently referenced in prophecy.

title: Moses in the Bulrushes
description: Pharaoh ordered all Hebrew baby boys thrown into the Nile, but Moses' mother placed him in a waterproof basket among the reeds of the river. Pharaoh's daughter discovered him and adopted him, raising him in the Egyptian palace. God used this unlikely beginning to prepare His deliverer of Israel.

title: Joshua's Altar on Mount Ebal
description: Following Moses' instructions, Joshua built an altar of uncut stones on Mount Ebal, offered burnt offerings, and inscribed a copy of the Law on the stones. All Israel gathered — half on Mount Gerizim and half on Mount Ebal — as Joshua read the blessings and curses of the Law.

title: Cyrus's Decree to Rebuild Jerusalem
description: In the first year of his reign, Cyrus king of Persia issued a decree allowing the Jewish exiles to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple. This fulfilled Jeremiah's 70-year prophecy and Isaiah's earlier prophecy that named Cyrus as God's anointed shepherd, over 150 years before Cyrus was born.

title: Queen Esther Saves the Jews
description: In the Persian capital of Susa, Queen Esther risked her life to approach King Xerxes and expose Haman's plot to destroy the Jewish people. Through her courage and wise planning, the Jews were saved from annihilation, and the Feast of Purim was established to commemorate their deliverance.

title: Nehemiah's Commissioning in Susa
description: Nehemiah served as cupbearer to King Artaxerxes in Susa. Upon hearing of Jerusalem's broken walls, he prayed and then requested permission to return and rebuild. The king granted his request, demonstrating God's faithfulness to restore His people and their holy city.

title: Barnabas, the Son of Encouragement
description: Cyprus was the homeland of Joseph, whom the apostles called Barnabas (Son of Encouragement). He sold his field and gave the proceeds to the church. Barnabas played a crucial role in accepting Paul into the church and later accompanied Paul on his first missionary journey, which began by preaching in Cyprus.

title: Titus's Ministry in Crete
description: Paul left Titus in Crete to appoint elders in every town and to set in order what was unfinished. Paul's letter to Titus gives instructions for church leadership and emphasizes sound doctrine and good deeds. The Cretan church was known for its challenges, as Paul quoted the Cretan poet Epimenides.

title: The Macedonian Call
description: While in Troas, Paul received a vision during the night of a man from Macedonia pleading, 'Come over to Macedonia and help us.' Paul and his companions immediately prepared to leave for Macedonia, understanding that God had called them to preach the gospel in Europe for the first time.

title: Paul's Ministry in Thessalonica
description: Paul preached in the synagogue at Thessalonica for three Sabbaths, proving from Scripture that Jesus was the Messiah. Many Greeks and prominent women believed, but jealous Jews started a riot. Paul and Silas escaped to Berea, but the church in Thessalonica grew strong despite persecution.

title: Paul Heals a Crippled Man in Lystra
description: In Lystra, Paul healed a man who had been crippled from birth. When the crowds saw what Paul had done, they shouted in Lycaonian, 'The gods have come down to us in human form!' They called Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes, and tried to offer sacrifices to them, which Paul and Barnabas vehemently refused.

title: Paul and Barnabas Preach in Derbe
description: After being stoned and left for dead in Lystra, Paul traveled to Derbe with Barnabas where they preached the gospel and many became disciples. They then returned through the cities they had visited, strengthening the disciples and appointing elders in every church.

title: Jonah Flees to Tarshish
description: When God commanded Jonah to preach against Nineveh, he instead fled in the opposite direction toward Tarshish, likely a distant port in Spain. He boarded a ship at Joppa, but God sent a great storm. The sailors threw Jonah overboard, and he was swallowed by a great fish.

title: Joshua Conquers Hazor
description: Jabin, king of Hazor, assembled a huge coalition of Canaanite kings to fight against Israel at the waters of Merom. Joshua and his army launched a surprise attack, and the Lord delivered them into Israel's hand. Joshua burned Hazor, which had been the head of all those kingdoms.

title: The Battle of Armageddon
description: Megiddo, from which the word Armageddon (Har Megiddo) derives, is prophesied as the site of the final battle between the forces of good and evil. Throughout history, many decisive battles were fought at Megiddo, including Deborah and Barak's victory over Sisera and King Josiah's death.

title: Samson's Wars with the Philistines
description: Samson judged Israel for 20 years, constantly battling the Philistines who dominated the coastal region of Philistia. He killed a thousand Philistines with a donkey's jawbone, carried away Gaza's gates, and ultimately destroyed the temple of Dagon, killing more Philistines in his death than in his life.

title: Solomon's Fleet at Ezion-Geber
description: King Solomon built a fleet of ships at Ezion-Geber, a port city on the Red Sea. From there, he sent expeditions to Ophir, bringing back gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks. Solomon's maritime trade brought great wealth but also led to alliances with foreign nations that turned his heart from God.

title: Ishmael in the Wilderness of Paran
description: After Sarah forced Hagar and her son Ishmael to leave, they wandered in the Wilderness of Beersheba. When their water ran out, Hagar placed Ishmael under a bush and wept. God heard the boy's cry and opened Hagar's eyes to a well. Ishmael grew up in the Wilderness of Paran and became an archer.

title: The Death of Miriam
description: Miriam, the sister of Moses and Aaron, died and was buried in Kadesh in the Wilderness of Zin. It was here also that the congregation had no water, leading to the incident where Moses struck the rock in disobedience. Miriam's death marked a significant loss for the Israelite leadership during the wilderness wanderings.

title: Noah's Sacrifice After the Flood
description: After leaving the ark on Mount Ararat, Noah built an altar and offered burnt offerings. God made a covenant with Noah, promising never again to destroy the earth with a flood and setting the rainbow in the sky as the sign of His promise. God blessed Noah and his sons to be fruitful and multiply.

title: The Dead Sea Scrolls
description: Near the Dead Sea, at Qumran, the Essene community preserved hundreds of ancient biblical manuscripts in caves. The Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered in the 1940s and 1950s, include copies of every Old Testament book except Esther, dating from the 3rd century BC to the 1st century AD. These discoveries confirmed the remarkable accuracy of biblical transmission.

title: The Tribe of Dan Conquers Laish
description: The tribe of Dan sent five spies who found the peaceful city of Laish in the far north, isolated from Sidonian influence. Six hundred armed Danites conquered the city, burned it with fire, and rebuilt it, naming it Dan after their ancestor. The city's conquest fulfilled the tribal allotment assigned by Joshua.

title: Jeroboam's Golden Calf at Dan
description: After the kingdom of Israel split, King Jeroboam set up a golden calf at Dan to prevent the northern tribes from worshiping in Jerusalem. He appointed non-Levitical priests and established a rival religious festival. Dan became a center of idolatry that the prophets repeatedly condemned as 'the sin of Jeroboam.'

title: From Dan to Beersheba
description: The city of Dan frequently served as the northern boundary of Israel, paired with Beersheba in the south. The phrase 'from Dan to Beersheba' appears throughout Scripture to describe the full extent of the Promised Land — from the heights of Mount Hermon to the Negev desert.
